title: Henrietta Lacks and HeLa Cells: Biospecimens, Informed Consent, and Genomic Privacy
text:
Who: Henrietta Lacks (1920–1951), a Black tobacco farmer, mother of five, and patient at Johns Hopkins Hospital.
What: Leftover tissue from her cervical biopsy was cultured without her consent, establishing the world’s first immortal cell line (HeLa), which became the foundation of modern biomedical research while her family remained in poverty.
Where / When: Johns Hopkins Hospital, Baltimore, Maryland, 1951 to the present.
Why it matters: The case highlights the ethical conflict between patient bodily autonomy, the requirement of informed consent, and the commercial exploitation of biospecimens. It exposes how rapid scientific advancement can run parallel to structural and racial inequities in clinical research.
Pair with: Research Ethics (Protecting Research Subjects)

title: Henrietta Lacks and HeLa Cells: Biospecimens, Informed Consent, and Genomic Privacy
text:
The Case
In January 1951, a thirty-year-old Black woman named Henrietta Lacks walked into the outpatient clinic of Johns Hopkins Hospital in Baltimore. She was bleeding heavily and felt a “knot” in her womb. Henrietta was a tobacco farmer’s daughter from southern Virginia who had moved to Maryland with her husband and five children to find work in the steel mills. Johns Hopkins was the only major hospital in the region that admitted Black patients, and even there, they were treated in segregated wards, separated from white patients by physical barriers and distinct medical teams.
Henrietta was examined by Dr. Howard Jones, a gynecologist who observed a deep, purple, glistening mass on her cervix—unlike any tumor he had seen before. He took a small tissue snippet and sent it to the pathology lab. The diagnosis was swift and devastating: an aggressive, malignant cervical adenocarcinoma.

Cervical Adenocarcinoma and Biopsy. Cervical adenocarcinoma is a cancer that begins in the glandular cells of the cervix. A biopsy is a standard diagnostic procedure where a small piece of tissue is surgically removed from a patient’s body so that pathologists can examine it under a microscope to identify malignant cells.

A few weeks later, Henrietta returned to the hospital to begin treatment. In 1951, the standard of care for cervical cancer was radium therapy: tubes of highly radioactive radium were placed in cloth pouches and sewn directly onto the patient’s cervix for several days to burn away the tumor. While Henrietta lay anesthetized on the operating table, the surgeon in charge, Dr. Lawrence Wharton Jr., performed the radium insertion. Before doing so, however, Wharton took two small circular tissue samples from Henrietta’s cervix—one from her active tumor, and one from healthy tissue nearby.
This action represented a crucial boundary crossing in clinical ethics. Dr. Wharton was acting as Henrietta’s primary clinician, responsible for delivering clinical care aimed directly at helping the patient. Yet, he took the tissue samples solely for clinical research at the request of Dr. George Gey, the head of tissue culture research at Johns Hopkins, whose goal was to produce generalizable knowledge (Emanuel et al. 2000). Wharton did not explain this distinction to Henrietta, nor did he ask for her permission.

Radium Therapy and Radium Insertions. In the early-to-mid twentieth century, radium therapy was a common form of cancer treatment. Physicians placed radioactive radium directly inside or next to a tumor (a procedure known as brachytherapy) to deliver high doses of localized radiation to destroy cancerous cells, frequently causing severe physical burns and systemic side effects.

George Gey had spent nearly three decades attempting to grow human cells in his laboratory. At the time, every scientist who tried to culture human cells encountered the same barrier: the cells would divide a few times and then die. Gey wanted to find an “immortal” human cell line—a line of cells that would grow and replicate indefinitely in a test tube, allowing researchers to study human biology without relying on living subjects.
Wharton dropped Henrietta’s tissue samples into a sterile tube and sent them to Gey’s lab. Gey’s assistant, Mary Kubicek, sliced the tumor tissue into tiny squares, placed them in a growth medium of chicken plasma, bovine embryo extract, and special umbilical cord blood, and sealed them in rotating tubes.
Unlike every other human sample Gey had cultured, Henrietta’s cells did not die. They grew with astonishing speed, doubling their population every twenty-four hours. They grew so aggressively that they consumed the nutrients in their tubes and began taking over adjacent cultures. Gey named the new cell line HeLa, using the first two letters of Henrietta’s first and last names.

Immortal Cell Line. An immortal cell line is a population of cells from a multicellular organism that, due to genetic mutations (such as those found in cancer), can escape normal cellular aging and continue dividing indefinitely under laboratory conditions, provided they are supplied with nutrients.

While Henrietta’s cultured cells flourished in the laboratory, Henrietta herself deteriorated. The radium therapy failed to stop the cancer. The aggressive adenocarcinoma metastasized throughout her abdominal cavity. She was admitted to the segregated wards of Johns Hopkins, where she suffered agonizing pain until her death on October 4, 1951, at the age of thirty-one. She was buried in an unmarked grave in rural Virginia.
The social and political environment of 1951 placed Henrietta in a position of acute vulnerability. Applying Kipnis’s taxonomy of research vulnerability, she suffered from profound deferential vulnerability: the rigid segregation of the Jim Crow era and the deep power asymmetries between elite, white male physicians and poor Black patients created a climate where questioning medical authority was socially and practically impossible.
George Gey did not keep the HeLa cells to himself. Recognizing their immense value to science, he began shipping vials of HeLa to researchers worldwide. HeLa cells became an indispensable tool of modern medicine. They were used to culture the polio virus, enabling Jonas Salk to develop the polio vaccine. They were launched into space to study the effects of zero gravity on human cells. They became the first cells to be cloned, the first to be mapped, and were used to develop chemotherapy, gene mapping, and in vitro fertilization techniques.

Biospecimen. A biospecimen is a biological sample of material (such as tissue, blood, urine, or cells) taken from a human body. Biospecimens are stored in biobanks and utilized by researchers to study diseases, develop drugs, and understand genetic pathways.

For twenty-five years, Henrietta’s family had no idea her cells were still alive. In the early 1970s, researchers realized that HeLa cells were so hardy they had contaminated other cell cultures across the globe. To map the HeLa genome and isolate the contamination, scientists tracked down Henrietta’s children—who had grown up in poverty in East Baltimore—to ask for blood samples.
When the researchers approached the family, they failed to explain the distinction between research and care. Consequently, Henrietta’s children experienced a classic therapeutic misconception: because they lacked formal education (cognitive vulnerability) and had no health insurance (allocational vulnerability), they believed the doctors were performing clinical tests to protect them from the hereditary cancer that had killed their mother, rather than gathering basic scientific data to sort out laboratory contamination.
Only when a family friend happened to mention HeLa cells did the Lacks family realize that Henrietta’s cells had been sold by the millions for research, yielding massive profits for biotech companies. Meanwhile, Henrietta’s five children struggled to pay for basic healthcare, and one son was homeless on the streets of Baltimore.
The Lacks family received no share of the billions of dollars in revenue generated by the commercialization of HeLa cells, nor were they ever compensated for the decades of unauthorized research performed on Henrietta’s biological material (Skloot 2010). The case remains a foundational failure of basic consent, exposing how rapid scientific advancement can run parallel to structural and racial inequities in clinical research.

title: Henrietta Lacks and HeLa Cells: Biospecimens, Informed Consent, and Genomic Privacy
text:
The Argument Henrietta Lacks Started
Few cases have generated as much widespread agreement that a historical injustice took place, combined with as much deep disagreement about what the ethical wrong actually was. The moves below trace that debate by engaging directly with real arguments from bioethics and legal literature.

Move 1: The Self-Ownership and Bodily Property Claim
The most intuitive response to the HeLa case is one of individual property rights and self-ownership. In her book Property in the Body: Feminist Perspectives, bioethicist Donna Dickenson (Dickenson 2007) argues that human biological material should not be treated as res nullius (nobody’s property) until a researcher “improves” it. Dickenson contends that treating human tissue as unowned waste until commercialized by biotechnology represents a form of asymmetric exploitation. Under this view, if biotechnology companies can hold property rights (such as patents) on cell lines, denying patients a corresponding property right in their own biological tissue creates an unjust legal double standard that disproportionately exploits female and minority bodies.

Informed Consent. Informed consent is the voluntary agreement of a competent individual to participate in a clinical trial or undergo a medical procedure, granted after the individual has been fully informed of the risks, benefits, alternatives, and scientific purpose of the intervention.


The Bodily Property Argument (Feminist Property Theory)

Every human being has a right of self-ownership over their own body, including their cells.
If humans have a right of self-ownership over their cells, then using a patient’s cells without consent and profit-sharing is a violation of self-ownership.
Therefore, utilizing a patient’s cells for commercial research without consent violates their right of self-ownership.


Under this argument, the remedy is clear: patients must have legal title to their excised tissues, giving them the right to control their use and share in any commercial profits.


Move 2: The Public Interest Objection and the Anticommons
The Bodily Property Argument was legally tested and rejected by the California Supreme Court in the landmark case Moore v. Regents of the University of California (1990) (Supreme Court of California 1990). The court was forced to balance patient autonomy against the broader public interest of scientific progress.
Writing for the majority, Justice Edward Panelli (Supreme Court of California 1990) argued that granting patients permanent property rights over their excised tissues would threaten the entire biomedical enterprise. Panelli relied heavily on the economic theory of the tragedy of the anticommons—a state where scientific progress is paralyzed because researchers must negotiate costly licensing agreements with thousands of tissue donors for every drug trial.

The Anticommons Argument (Justice Panelli’s Majority Opinion)

Public policy should maximize scientific progress in medicine for the public good.
Granting patients property rights over excised tissues would create an “anticommons” that paralyzes scientific progress.
Therefore, public policy should not grant patients property rights over excised tissues.


However, this pragmatic view was sharply contested within the court itself. In a famous dissenting opinion, Justice Stanley Mosk (Supreme Court of California 1990) rejected the majority’s asymmetric view of property. Mosk argued that if researchers and biotechnology companies can hold commercial property rights (patents) in a patient’s cell line, denying the patient any reciprocal share in that property is arbitrary, unequal, and unjust.

The Reciprocal Property Argument (Justice Mosk’s Dissenting Opinion)

It is unjust to grant biotech companies commercial property rights in cell lines while denying any property rights to the patients who provided the cells.
Biotechnology companies are legally granted commercial property rights in cell lines.
Therefore, patients should be granted a corresponding, equitable property interest in their cells.


According to Mosk, the public interest does not require exploiting patients. To allow corporations to enrich themselves while leaving the patient with nothing is a failure of basic legal equity.


Move 3: Relational Justice and Systemic Exploitation
The dispute between Panelli and Mosk focuses heavily on property law. But in her book Medical Apartheid, bioethicist Harriet Washington (Washington 2006) argues that focusing solely on transaction failures (such as whether Henrietta was paid) misses the deeper structural injustice. Washington places the HeLa case within a long, systemic history of medical exploitation targeting African Americans.
Under the Belmont principle of Justice (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979), the ethical wrong is a relational and structural one: a medical system that extracts biological resources from marginalized, under-insured populations to generate private corporate wealth, while returning no clinical benefits or basic healthcare access to those same communities.

The Relational Justice Argument (Harriet Washington’s Critique)

Excised biological tissue remains morally connected to the patient’s personal and genetic identity.
Profiting from a patient’s genetic identity while denying their family access to basic healthcare treats them as a mere tool for another’s wealth.
Therefore, commercializing a patient’s tissue without clinical benefit or compensation is exploitative and unjust.


From the relational justice perspective, the remedy is not to turn patients into tissue merchants, but to mandate community benefit sharing, corporate accountability, and universal access to the healthcare HeLa helped create.


Move 4: The Broad Consent Framework (The Regulatory Compromise)
In 2018, the US Department of Health and Human Services attempted to resolve this ongoing dispute by rewriting the Common Rule (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)—the federal policy protecting human subjects. The updated regulations introduced the concept of broad consent.
Bioethicists like Ezekiel Emanuel (Emanuel et al. 2000) defended this regulatory move as a necessary compromise. It attempts to respect the Belmont principle of Respect for Persons (autonomy) at the point of clinical entry, while protecting Beneficence (scientific progress) by avoiding the paralyzing transaction costs of re-contacting patients for every subsequent biobank experiment.

Figure 1: The modern regulatory workflow for tissue banking under the 2018 Common Rule. When clinical biospecimens are collected (Left), they can be stored and used for future research only if they are de-identified (Middle). If the tissue is de-identified, the regulatory system licenses future unspecified research provided the patient checked the ‘Broad Consent’ box at clinical intake (Right). This workflow represents a compromise: it respects autonomy via an initial opt-in, but avoids the scientific paralysis of requiring specific consent for every subsequent experiment.






Broad Consent. Broad consent is an authorization framework introduced under the 2018 Common Rule. It allows a patient to give a single, overarching consent at clinical intake for unspecified future scientific research on their stored biological tissues and data, bypassing the need for researchers to contact the patient for every new study.


The Regulatory Compromise Argument

Tissue research regulations must protect patient autonomy while preserving scientific efficiency.
A system of “broad consent” at clinical intake protects autonomy through an initial choice while preserving scientific efficiency by avoiding re-consent.
Therefore, broad consent is the most ethically balanced regulatory framework for tissue banking.


Proponents of this move argue that had broad consent existed in 1951, Henrietta would have been given the choice to opt-in, resolving the ethical failure while preserving George Gey’s ability to distribute HeLa cells to global research laboratories.


Move 5: Big Data, AI, and the Genomic Privacy Turn
The broad consent compromise rests entirely on one crucial premise: de-identification. Under the Common Rule, if a biospecimen is stripped of names, medical record numbers, and other identifying details, it is no longer considered “human subjects research,” and the strict consent rules cease to apply.
But in the age of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence (AI), de-identification has become a myth. In 2013, researchers led by Jonathan Landry et al. (Landry et al. 2013) sequenced the full HeLa genome and published it publicly on the internet. Because a person’s genome is uniquely theirs, publishing Henrietta’s genetic sequence instantly exposed private medical liabilities and disease risks about her living children, grandchildren, and future descendants. The family was never asked.
Writing in Nature, then-NIH Director Francis Collins (Hudson and Collins 2013) led the response, negotiating a historic agreement to place HeLa genomic data under controlled access. But as bioethicists like Collins noted, this controversy exposed a deep systemic flaw: genomic data is fundamentally relational shared data. It belongs to a biological family, not just an individual. One person’s decision to donate their genetic sample or allow its de-identified sequencing automatically compromises the privacy of their relatives.
Today, Machine Learning (ML) models and AI-driven drug discovery have transformed this debate into a live ethical crisis:

AI-Driven Re-identification: Modern AI algorithms can easily cross-reference supposedly “de-identified” genomic sequences in biobanks with public genetic genealogy databases (like GEDmatch) and electronic health records, stripping away patient anonymity.
AI Training and Biological IP: Pharmaceutical companies now train massive machine learning models on millions of patients’ biospecimen sequences to discover drugs. If an AI model learns its weights from Henrietta’s genetic sequence or HeLa-derived proteins, who owns the resulting neural network? Does the donor estate have a claim on an AI weight file trained on their biological material?


The Genomic Identity Argument

If genomic data can be easily re-identified, then researchers cannot rely on “de-identification” to bypass informed consent.
Modern machine learning can easily re-identify genomic datasets.
Therefore, researchers cannot rely on de-identification to bypass informed consent for genomic research.


This is the live edge of the dialogue. Today, we give blood, tumor biopsies, and saliva samples, believing they are “de-identified.” Yet, big data and AI can easily cross-reference these samples to re-identify us. The argument Henrietta Lacks started in 1951 is not a historical artifact—it is a live bioethical conflict that directly impacts our own genetic privacy and bodily rights today.

title: Henrietta Lacks and HeLa Cells: Biospecimens, Informed Consent, and Genomic Privacy
text:
Discussion Questions

Reconstruct & Pressure-Test: Reconstruct The Anticommons Argument (Panelli’s majority view) and The Reciprocal Property Argument (Mosk’s dissenting view) in your own words. Identify the single, core legal or moral premise that these two arguments disagree on, and defend one side of the dispute.
Evaluating Broad Consent: If Henrietta Lacks had signed a modern “broad consent” form in 1951, would this have resolved the structural and relational injustices raised by Harriet Washington? Why or why not?
The Ethics of AI Training: Biotechnology companies are currently training generative AI models on clinical and genomic datasets to design new synthetic proteins. If a patient’s biological data is used to train an AI model, does the patient hold any moral claim to the resulting model weights or intellectual property? Use the Belmont principles to defend your answer.
Clinical Practice Roleplay: You are an oncology nurse. A patient undergoing a tumor biopsy asks you: “I saw a box on my clinical forms about donating my left-over tissue. If they find something valuable in my cells and sell it, will my family get any money or help with our medical bills?” How do you explain the clinical, legal, and financial realities of tissue donation without discouraging research?
Genomic Privacy: Given that genomic data cannot be truly de-identified and directly implicates a person’s children and relatives, should family members have a veto right over an individual’s decision to donate their genetic samples to public research databases?

title: Henrietta Lacks and HeLa Cells: Biospecimens, Informed Consent, and Genomic Privacy
text:
Further Reading

Book: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks by Rebecca Skloot (2010). The definitive, exhaustively researched narrative that brings Henrietta’s personal story and the scientific history of HeLa cells together.
Book: Medical Apartheid: The Dark History of Medical Experimentation on Black Americans from Colonial Times to the Present by Harriet A. Washington (2006). A crucial historical text exploring structural racism and exploitation in medical research.
Article: “Biospecimen Policy: Family Matters” by Kathy L. Hudson and Francis S. Collins (2013) in Nature, detailing the historical NIH-Lacks family agreement and genomic database structure.

title: Henrietta Lacks and HeLa Cells: Biospecimens, Informed Consent, and Genomic Privacy

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Who: Donald Herbert “Dax” Cowart (1947–2019), a 25-year-old former Air Force pilot from Henderson, Texas
What: Burned over roughly two-thirds of his body in a 1973 propane explosion; he repeatedly, and competently, demanded that treatment stop and was overruled for more than a year
Where / When: Parkland Memorial Hospital (Dallas), a rehabilitation hospital in Houston, and John Sealy Hospital at the University of Texas Medical Branch (Galveston), 1973–1974
Why it matters: Cowart’s case became the most-discussed American example of a collision between patient autonomy (a competent person’s right to refuse treatment) and clinical beneficence (the duty to act for the patient’s good). Even after a full recovery and a successful career, he insisted his refusal should have been honored. The argument he started has run for fifty years and is not over.
Pair with: History & the Clinical Relationship

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
The Case
On the afternoon of July 25, 1973, Dax Cowart and his father, Ray, drove out to look at a piece of rural land in east Texas. Dax — then christened Donald, a name he would later change — was twenty-five, a former high-school football captain, a rodeo rider, and an Air Force pilot recently back from service. He was, by every account including his own, a young man who took his strong, fast, capable body for granted.
They did not know that an underground propane pipeline near where they parked had been leaking, and that the gas had pooled, invisible and odorless to them, in the low ground around the car. When Ray turned the ignition to leave, a spark from the engine ignited it. The explosion threw Dax clear of a wall of fire. He ran through pine woods until he could not run anymore, beating at flames with hands that were already destroyed.
A farmer reached him first. Cowart’s first recorded words, to that stranger, were a request for a weapon: “Get me a gun. Can’t you see I’m a dead man. I’m going to die anyway.” Both men were taken to a small hospital in Kilgore, then transferred together — 140 miles, in the same ambulance — to the burn unit at Parkland Memorial Hospital in Dallas. Ray Cowart died on the way. Dax survived. Burns covered about 65% of his body; the deepest, third-degree burns destroyed the skin of his face, ears, and hands. He was blinded in both eyes. Most of his fingers were later amputated. He was conscious, and he stayed conscious.
What followed was fourteen months of treatment that Cowart would describe, decades later and without exaggeration, as “pure hell.” The medical necessity was real.

Why the treatment was unavoidable. Burn depth is graded: first-degree burns affect only the outer skin (an ordinary sunburn); second-degree burns blister and are intensely painful; third-degree (full-thickness) burns destroy all skin layers. The amount burned is reported as percent of total body surface area (TBSA). At ~65% TBSA, much of it third-degree, Cowart was at the edge of what was survivable in 1973, and survival required debridement — the physical removal of dead tissue, because new skin cannot grow over it and dead tissue breeds lethal infection. This was often done in a Hubbard tank, a steel pool of warm antiseptic water that softened the dead skin so it could be scrubbed and cut away. The procedure works. Before modern pain control, it was also one of the most agonizing routine treatments in medicine.

From very early on — at Parkland, and at every stage afterward — he asked the people treating him to stop. Not in passing, and not only when the pain was worst. He asked his doctors, his nurses, and his mother, repeatedly and consistently, to discontinue treatment and let him die. He understood what he was asking for. He understood that without the tankings and skin grafts he would die of infection within weeks. He wanted that outcome over the one being forced on him. He felt, he said, that he was “being skinned alive.”
A psychiatrist, Dr. Robert B. White, was brought in to evaluate him at Galveston. The implied hope was that White would find Cowart too depressed or too overwhelmed to be making a real decision — which would have authorized treating him against his will with a clear conscience. White concluded the opposite. Cowart, he found, was “not in the least incompetent or mentally ill.” It was during this period that White recorded the interview that became the 1974 film Please Let Me Die — a competent man, on camera, lucidly asking to be allowed to die, and being treated anyway (White 1974).
The most revealing turn in the story is one the standard retellings often skip. After about eight months at Parkland, Cowart was moved to a rehabilitation hospital in Houston. There he again refused further treatment — and the rehabilitation physician accepted the refusal. For a moment, the system did what Cowart asked. Then his mother, Ada, his legal next of kin, and his attorney intervened and had him transferred to the burn unit at John Sealy Hospital in Galveston, where treatment — and the tankings — resumed under the unit’s director, Dr. Duane Larson. The decisive fact of the case is not that no one would listen. Someone did. He was moved away from the person who listened.
He survived, was discharged blind and severely disabled, and was, by his own description, deeply depressed for years; he attempted suicide more than once. Then his life changed. He earned a law degree from Texas Tech University in 1986, married, practiced law, and became one of the most articulate patient-rights advocates in the country. He died on April 28, 2019, at seventy-one, of complications from leukemia and liver cancer.
For the rest of that life he said two things that many listeners could not hold together. He was glad to be alive. And his physicians had been wrong to keep him alive against his will. He did not think these statements contradicted each other. The good outcome, he insisted, was never the question. The question was whose decision it had been — and that question became one of the longest-running arguments in modern bioethics.

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
The Argument Dax Started
What makes Cowart’s case central to bioethics is not the horror of the burns but the quality of the disagreement it produced. Over fifty years, Cowart, his physicians, and a series of philosophers and lawyers built an argument, attacked it, repaired it, and attacked the repair. The pieces below trace that exchange as a connected dialogue — each move answering the one before.

The argument from autonomy
Cowart’s own position is the cleanest. He insisted the issue was control over his body, not the value of the result: “the right to control your own body is a right you’re born with, not something that you have to ask anyone else for, not the government, not your treating physician, not your next-of-kin” (Cowart and Burt 1998).

The Autonomy Argument

A competent adult has a moral right to refuse any medical treatment.
Dax Cowart was a competent adult who refused treatment.
Therefore, treating Cowart over his objection violated his moral rights.


Premise 1 is now close to settled doctrine; courts and ethicists broadly accept that bodily self-determination includes the right to decline care. So the argument’s whole weight falls on premise 2 — and that is exactly where his physicians attacked it.

Capacity, competence, and the surrogate. Decision-making capacity is a clinical judgment that a patient can (1) understand the relevant information, (2) appreciate how it applies to them, (3) reason about the options, and (4) communicate a choice. It is decision-specific and time-specific. “Competence” is the parallel legal term. When a patient lacks capacity, a surrogate (often next of kin) decides for them — but a surrogate has no authority to override a patient who still has capacity. Two psychiatrists found Cowart had capacity; his mother’s authorization was nonetheless treated as controlling. That is the legal pressure point premise 2 exposes.



The beneficence reply: pain, shock, and “they almost always change their minds”
The physicians did not deny premise 1. They denied premise 2 — not that Cowart could talk coherently, but that a person in catastrophic pain, in shock, facing a future he could not yet imagine, is making a settled, autonomous choice rather than crying out from an unbearable present. Dr. Larson, on the film Dax’s Case, put both the conviction and its limit in one breath: “No other burn patient has made it so clear … that they really do desire to die. … This is an ongoing battle that we have to individualize.”

The Beneficence / Temporary-Incapacity Reply

If a patient is in extreme pain and shock, their capacity to refuse treatment is undermined.
Dax Cowart was in extreme pain and shock.
Therefore, Dax Cowart lacked the capacity to refuse treatment.


This is essentially the position the philosopher H. Tristram Engelhardt later defended in the Dax’s Case essays as a genuine conflict “at the roots of health care” between freedom and best interest (Kliever 1989). It also has empirical backing that did not exist when commentators first took Cowart’s side. A 1978 NIH consensus statement held that shock makes early decision-making nearly impossible for burn victims, and in a later study burn survivors interviewed years afterward said almost uniformly that informed consent was unrealistic at the moment of injury, that the capacity to consent developed over time, and that none thought withdrawing support would have been right for them (Brewster et al. 2006).

Informed consent vs. informed refusal — and how new it was. Informed consent — disclosure plus free agreement before treatment — was barely a decade old in law in 1973; Canterbury v. Spence, which set the modern “reasonable patient” disclosure standard, had been decided only the year before (United States Court of Appeals, District of Columbia Circuit 1972). The harder idea, informed refusal (a competent patient’s right to decline life-saving care), was almost unrecognized. Many physicians trained in an older paternalistic model treated refusal of life-saving treatment as itself evidence of impaired judgment — which is precisely the reasoning premise 1 of the reply formalizes (see Faden and Beauchamp (1986)).

But the reply has a soft spot. Premise 2 is a statistical generalization — most patients are glad later — used to override this patient. Applying an average to the individual in front of you is a recognizably utilitarian move (maximize good outcomes across the class of burn patients), and it invites the objection Cowart spent his life pressing.


Cowart’s counter: a good outcome doesn’t make the choice yours
Cowart’s reply is not “you were wrong about the outcome.” He concedes the outcome. His reply is that a good result does not retroactively transfer the decision to the people who made it.

The Retrospective-Endorsement Objection

The right to make a decision does not depend on the outcome of that decision.
Pointing to a patient’s later gladness is an appeal to the outcome.
Therefore, a patient’s later gladness does not prove that the decision belonged to the medical team.


He sharpened it with an analogy: a surgeon who operates on the wrong patient is not vindicated because the patient happens to recover. And this is where a concept from ethical theory does real work. Cowart’s two claims — I am glad to be alive and I was wronged — are not a contradiction; they are a textbook case of moral residue: even when overriding a duty is, on balance, defensible, the overridden duty does not vanish, and something wrongful remains to be acknowledged. As he said: “I do not feel grateful to anyone for fighting my request to die … no, I’m not glad they forcibly treated me because the pain that I went through was pure hell” (Cowart and Burt 1998). From a Kantian angle the same point is that treating a competent person merely as a means to a future others judge worthwhile fails to respect him as a person who may author his own life — even badly.


Burt’s relational counter: “I won’t abandon you”
The legal scholar Robert Burt, who debated Cowart publicly for years, accepted almost all of this and still resisted the conclusion that the team should simply have complied. His move changes the frame from a transaction (consent given or withheld) to a relationship.

The Relational Counter

Desperate demands to die are often cries for sustained relationship rather than pure refusals.
If a demand is a cry for relationship, then immediately complying with it is a form of abandonment.
Therefore, immediately honoring a patient’s refusal to live can be a form of abandonment.


This is recognizably an ethics of care position: obligations grow out of relationship and dependency, not only out of rights. It is also where the dialogue gets its most famous phrase. Burt kept asking when comes the end of the day? — conceding that staying cannot be indefinite, because a relationship that never lets the patient’s “no” be final has quietly become the old paternalism in kinder language. Cowart’s answer was blunt: “an hour was an eternity, with the burns and the pain I was going through.” Burt’s honest reply was that he did not always know. That unresolved gap is what the next move tries to close.


The Arnold–Menzel procedure: turning principles into a process
Denis Arnold and Paul Menzel took the Burt–Cowart standoff seriously as an unanswered question — when, exactly, does resistance to a competent refusal have to yield? — and proposed a step-by-step procedure to answer it without smuggling paternalism back in. Their guiding question reframes the whole debate away from absolute rights: not “who holds the trump card?” but “Whose pain is it, anyway?” — a question, they write, about who may decide that agonizing pain now is worth a possible good life later (Arnold and Menzel 1998).
The procedure is, in the vocabulary of principlism, an exercise in specification and balancing: it takes two abstract principles that conflict in the abstract — respect for autonomy and beneficence — and turns them into a concrete sequence a real clinical team can follow at the bedside. The decisive split is whether the patient’s pain is being adequately controlled, because uncontrolled pain both clouds judgment and is itself a harm the patient is being forced to endure. Read the diagram as a single question that forks: first fix the pain or honor a short waiting period; either way, the patient — not the team and not the family — ends up holding the decision.

adequately managed?

Figure 1: The Arnold–Menzel “end of the day” procedure. Burt asked when a care team must finally stop resisting a competent patient’s refusal; Arnold and Menzel answer with a process rather than a fixed deadline. The pivotal question (gold diamond) is whether severe pain is controlled. If it is, the team owes the patient an honest prognosis, an agreed decision date, and an advocate, and may decline the refusal only for a short period (about a week). If pain is not controlled, the team may hold only long enough to set and pursue an explicit pain-control deadline. On both paths the endpoint (green) is the same: the competent patient controls the decision. Adapted from Arnold & Menzel (1998).







The argument that hasn’t ended: the empirical turn
Cowart’s case itself never produced a court ruling; the legal architecture grew up around it. In re Quinlan (1976) recognized that life-sustaining treatment could be withdrawn through a surrogate (Supreme Court of New Jersey 1976), and Cruzan v. Director (1990) treated the refusal of such treatment as a protected liberty while letting states demand clear evidence of the patient’s wishes (Supreme Court of the United States 1990); out of that lineage came living wills, durable powers of attorney, and the federal Patient Self-Determination Act. The law moved Cowart’s way. The bedside did not always follow: in a 2019 remembrance, the bioethicist Greg Pence reported a burn physician saying, of patients who ask to die, “They all want to die, and I just ignore them. I don’t let them give up on themselves” (Parsi and Winslade 2019). Advance directives, when they exist, are quietly overridden a large share of the time. The doctrine settled; the disagreement did not.
If the story stopped with Arnold and Menzel, the lesson would still look tidy: respect autonomy, control pain, hand the patient the decision. The most important recent move complicates exactly that lesson — and it comes not from law but from data. The bioethicist Monica Gerrek argues that building burn ethics almost entirely on Cowart has spread a belief — that burn units are inherently paternalistic — that the wider evidence does not support (Gerrek 2018). She sets beside Dax the case of Andrea Rubin, severely burned in 2014 with comparable injuries. Rubin’s friends, like Dax, pleaded that she would not want to live this way; her father, like Dax’s mother, consented; she was sedated for weeks and could not decide. After recovery, Rubin did not feel betrayed. She believes she genuinely lacked capacity early on, that the team acted in her interest, and that honoring her friends’ pleas would have been a fatal mistake.
Rubin’s case does not refute Cowart — two psychiatrists found he had capacity, and hers is also just one story. What it does is reopen premise 2 of the original autonomy argument as a genuinely empirical question that cannot be settled from a single famous case: capacity in catastrophic injury is harder to read, and more variable across people, than either “burn units are tyrannical” or “patients always come around” allows. Cowart himself, asked how a physician could ever be sure a patient truly wants to die, conceded: “I doubt that there is any way a physician can be absolutely sure.” Fifty years on, that is roughly where the argument rests — not at an answer, but at a sharper question, with Cowart’s autonomy claim intact and the certainty once used against it gone on both sides.

title: Dax Cowart and the Right to Refuse Life-Saving Treatment: Autonomy, Beneficence, and Paternalism
text:
Discussion Questions

Reconstruct The Autonomy Argument and The Beneficence / Temporary-Incapacity Reply in your own words. They share no disputed premise except one — identify it, and say what kind of evidence (clinical, philosophical, statistical) could actually settle it.
Cowart said he was glad to be alive and that his rights had been violated. Using the idea of moral residue, explain why this is not a contradiction — and say what it implies for how we should judge paternalistic decisions that “turned out fine.”
The Beneficence Reply uses a statistical generalization (“most such patients are later glad”) to override one individual. When, if ever, is it legitimate to apply a true generalization about a group to the particular person in front of you? What principle are you relying on?
You are the nurse assigned to Cowart’s daily tank treatment. He has just told you, again, that he wants it to stop and wants to die. Drawing on the virtues of a good nurse and Burt’s relational counter, what do you owe him in that moment that the institution does not — and what, concretely, do you do?
Apply the Arnold–Menzel procedure to Andrea Rubin’s case. Does the procedure yield the outcome Rubin endorses? If it does not, is that a flaw in the procedure, a difference between the two patients, or evidence that the argument Dax started is still unresolved?

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Who: ~600 Black men of Macon County, Alabama — 399 with latent syphilis, 201 uninfected controls — and, later, more than 1,300 Guatemalans deliberately infected by the same U.S. agency
What: The U.S. Public Health Service watched untreated syphilis progress to autopsy for 40 years (1932–1972), withholding penicillin and deceiving the men into believing they were being treated; a parallel PHS project in Guatemala (1946–1948) infected prisoners, soldiers, and psychiatric patients on purpose
Where / When: Tuskegee/Macon County, Alabama, 1932–1972; Guatemala, 1946–1948; exposed by a whistleblower and the press in 1972; the Guatemala records found in 2003
Why it matters: Tuskegee is widely called the single most important event in the rise of American bioethics. It produced the modern rules — informed consent, the IRB, the Belmont principles — and a debate, still unsettled, about whether rules were ever the thing that was missing, and about the medical distrust the study left behind.
Pair with: History & the Making of Research Ethics

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
The Case
In 1932 the United States Public Health Service (PHS) — the federal agency that would become the CDC — began what its own scientists titled the Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis in the Negro Male. The premise was simple and, to its designers, scientifically attractive: find people with untreated syphilis and observe what the disease does to a human body if nothing is done, all the way to autopsy.
They chose Macon County, Alabama, where most residents were Black, most were poor, and almost none had access to a doctor. That was the point. The men recruited — about 600, of whom 399 had latent syphilis and 201 served as healthy controls — were sharecroppers working white-owned land in a system of debt peonage. The PHS enlisted the respected Tuskegee Institute and a trusted Black public-health nurse, Eunice Rivers, whose constancy kept the men coming back for decades. The men were never told they had syphilis. They were told they had “bad blood,” a vague local phrase, and that the government doctors were treating them for it.

What untreated syphilis does. Syphilis is a bacterial infection. After an early stage it can go latent — silent for years — and then, in roughly a third of untreated cases, attack the heart, blood vessels, brain, and spinal cord. The consequences the PHS’s own venereal-disease workers printed on a matchbook to scare people into testing were blunt and accurate: “Blindness, heart injury, insanity, death.” None of that was named to the Tuskegee men.

The deception was not passive. To collect the data they wanted, investigators performed painful diagnostic spinal taps and described them to the men as a special free “spinal shot” — a treatment. One PHS officer wrote to colleagues that the men might refuse the procedure if they understood it was only diagnostic, so its real purpose should “be kept from them as far as possible.” Because the study’s final data point was the autopsy, the PHS offered burial insurance — a powerful inducement in a poor rural community where a proper funeral mattered — so that families would surrender the bodies. One investigator wrote that the team had “no further interest in these patients until they die.” When a mobile treatment unit later swept the county offering free penicillin to everyone, PHS staff quietly flagged their enrollees to local doctors with the instruction that the man was under study and not to be treated.






Figure 1: A Public Health Service worker draws blood from a study participant during an annual “roundup,” Milstead, Macon County, 1953. Nurse Eunice Rivers stands at left.




National Archives and Records Administration. Public domain. Reproduced in (Tobin 2022).

Penicillin became the standard cure for syphilis in the mid-1940s. The men were not given it. PHS scientists argued that the arrival of a cure made the study more valuable, not less — a never-to-be-repeated chance to see the full natural course of a disease that would soon no longer run its course anywhere else. The study generated about fifteen papers in reputable medical journals over thirty-seven years. The titles alone announced what it was — one 1964 article was called “The Tuskegee Study of Untreated Syphilis; the 30th Year of Observation.” A 1955 paper reported that more than thirty percent of the autopsied men had died directly of syphilis. No physician, anywhere, published a letter of objection.
It ended only because one PHS employee would not let it go. By the most-cited later reckonings, dozens of the men had died of syphilis or its complications, dozens of their wives had been infected, and children had been born with congenital syphilis. In 1973, after the story broke, a class-action suit produced an out-of-court settlement, and the government eventually provided lifetime medical care to survivors and affected families. In 1997 — a quarter century after the study stopped — President Clinton apologized: what the government did “was shameful,” a study “so clearly racist.” No scientist was ever prosecuted.
The case is usually taught as a failure of consent. The questions it actually raises are harder than that, and they are still open.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
The Argument Tuskegee Started
Few cases have generated as much agreement that something monstrous happened and as much disagreement about what the monstrous thing was. The moves below trace that argument as a connected exchange — each answering the one before.

“We were missing rules”
The official lesson is institutional. Tuskegee, on this view, happened because the United States had no system requiring that subjects consent and no body to review research before it ran. So the country built one: the 1974 National Research Act, institutional review boards (IRBs) to approve and monitor studies, federal informed-consent regulations, and, in 1979, the Belmont Report’s three principles — respect for persons, beneficence, and justice.

Informed consent, the IRB, and Belmont. Informed consent means a competent person is told the truth about a study’s risks, benefits, and purpose and freely agrees to take part. An IRB is a committee that must approve human research before it begins and can stop it. The Belmont Report distilled the post-Tuskegee consensus into three duties: respect persons as autonomous agents, do not harm and maximize benefit, and distribute research’s burdens and benefits fairly. These are the spine of every research-ethics rulebook in use today (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979).


The Regulatory-Gap Argument

If a system lacks independent review and consent regulations, then research abuses like Tuskegee are inevitable.
Modern research systems now have independent review and consent regulations.
Therefore, research abuses like Tuskegee are no longer possible in modern research systems.


Premise 1 is where this breaks. It assumes the gap was the absence of rules. The next move denies that.


“The rules existed — and were ignored”
The Nuremberg Code, written in 1947 after Nazi doctors were tried for experimenting on prisoners, opens with the sentence “The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential.” It existed for the last twenty-five years of Tuskegee. American researchers of the era reportedly dismissed it as “a good code for barbarians” — something for Nazis, not for them. Rules were not missing. They were not felt to apply.
Then there is Guatemala. While researching Surgeon General Thomas Parran’s papers in 2003, the historian Susan Reverby opened the donated files of a PHS doctor named John Cutler and found something worse than Tuskegee (Reverby 2011). Between 1946 and 1948, the same Public Health Service had deliberately infected more than 1,300 Guatemalans — prisoners, soldiers, and patients in a psychiatric hospital — with syphilis, gonorrhea, and chancroid, to test prevention. When infection through arranged contact proved unreliable, investigators inoculated subjects directly. At least eighty-three deaths were recorded. One psychiatric patient, after months of induced disease, was dying when a researcher infected her further; she died days later.
The decisive fact about Guatemala is not its cruelty but its respectability. The principal investigator, John Mahoney, was arguably the most honored American physician-scientist of his day — he won a Lasker Award the year the experiments began. The proposal was reviewed and funded by an NIH study section staffed by physicians from the country’s leading medical schools. The Surgeon General was kept informed and enthusiastic; he is recorded remarking that “we couldn’t do such an experiment in this country.” Nothing failed in the chain of command. Everyone with authority approved.

Figure 2: The PHS chain of command in the Tuskegee and Guatemala studies. The point of the diagram is what it does not contain: a broken link. Approval and knowledge flowed through every legitimate level of authority — the Surgeon General, the funding review, the senior investigators, the trusted on-site nurse — down to instructing local doctors that enrolled men were ‘not to be treated.’ The one person who finally stopped Tuskegee, Peter Buxtun, sits outside this structure entirely (dashed). A rule is enforced by the people inside the boxes; that is why ‘add a rule’ did not, by itself, answer the problem.






The Conscience Argument

Rules only prevent abuse if researchers believe the rules apply to them and their subjects.
The PHS researchers did not believe existing ethical rules applied to their subjects.
Therefore, the failure was a failure of institutional and individual conscience, not a lack of rules.


That individual was Peter Buxtun. Hired by the PHS in 1965 as a venereal-disease interviewer in San Francisco, the Czech-born social worker — whose family had fled to America in 1937 — learned of the study from a coworker, obtained a folder of internal “roundup” reports, and recognized the shape of what he was reading. He had read the Nuremberg trial record. He filed formal objections in 1966 and again in 1968. The CDC summoned him to Atlanta and a senior official berated him. A blue-ribbon panel was convened in 1969; its dominant voice argued, “You will never have another study like this; take advantage of it,” and only one member, Gene Stollerman, urged that the men be treated as patients rather than as a data set. The panel voted to continue. Buxtun asked the CDC, in writing, “What is the ethical thing to do?” He received no answer. He took the documents to the press; the story broke in July 1972.






Figure 3: Peter Buxtun, the PHS social worker whose complaints, ignored inside the agency for years, finally reached the press in 1972.




Public domain. Reproduced in (Tobin 2022).

Why did it take one outsider seven years, when no insider acted in forty? Buxtun’s own answer was wry: “It’s tough being a whistleblower when you don’t even know you’re a whistleblower.” Stronger pressure to weave in here is from social psychology. Stanley Milgram’s obedience experiments — and a later study modeled on them in which university students were asked to recruit others into an obviously dangerous sham experiment — found that most people, given a legitimate-seeming authority, comply and look away, even when dissent is safe, anonymous, and easy (Elliott 2018). Milgram called this surrender the “agentic state”: we recast ourselves as instruments of the authority and let our conscience go quiet. The Tuskegee physicians were not monsters by Milgram’s lights; they were ordinary people inside a structure that made silence feel normal.


“But heroes are not a policy”
If the Conscience Argument is right, it leaves a problem. You cannot run a research system on the hope that each abuse will produce its own Buxtun — especially since the system punishes the ones it gets.

The Institutional-Design Reply

Relying on individual conscience only works if whistleblowers can safely come forward.
Under current institutional designs, whistleblowers who come forward are routinely punished.
Therefore, preventing future research abuses requires structural institutional reform, not just appeals to personal courage.


The constructive half of this comes, again, from Milgram: obedience dropped sharply when the authority figure was made less imposing, when the victim was made more present, and — most powerfully — when even one other person in the room dissented. The design implication is concrete: deflate the prestige of the lead researcher, make subjects visible rather than abstract, protect real (not cosmetic) channels for objection, and make it possible for objectors to find each other. The recurring real-world successes — the New Zealand “unfortunate experiment,” for instance — came from teams of clinicians acting together, not solitary martyrs. An anonymous “ethics hotline,” the usual institutional answer, is the thing the evidence trusts least.


Who was chosen, and why
There is a wrong here that none of the moves above quite names. The Tuskegee men were not random. They were selected because they were poor, Black, unschooled, and without doctors — chosen for the very powerlessness that made them easy to deceive and unlikely to be believed. A PHS internal phrasing reduced them to a single compound label, a string of demographic adjectives ending in “men.” That is not a consent failure or an oversight failure. It is a justice failure: a vulnerable group made to carry a burden for a science that would never have been done to the powerful.

Vulnerability and fair subject selection. Kenneth Kipnis argued that “vulnerable” is not a list of groups but a set of pressures a participant is under — economic, social, deferential, institutional — that compromise a genuinely free choice (Kipnis 2001). The Belmont principle of justice demands fair subject selection: research must not load its risks onto those least able to refuse, nor (the mirror error) exclude groups from research’s benefits. Tuskegee is the textbook case of wrongful exploitation of the vulnerable; modern ethics also watches for wrongful exclusion.


The Justice Reframing

In Tuskegee, subjects were selected specifically because their poverty and race made them unable to refuse or complain.
Selecting subjects because of their vulnerability is a form of exploitation that violates the principle of justice.
Therefore, the deepest wrong of Tuskegee was a violation of justice, which cannot be fixed merely by improving consent procedures.


This is not safely historical. Research today still gravitates toward the institutionalized, the incarcerated, the cognitively impaired, and the poor — people under exactly Kipnis’s pressures — which is why “fair subject selection” remains an active demand and not a settled box to check.


The argument that hasn’t ended
There is a final move, and it is the one with the most current stakes. The standard account says: Tuskegee is why many Black Americans distrust medicine and research, so the remedy is outreach, apology, and education to repair the trust the study broke. After 1972 the empirical legacy is, in fact, measurable — Marcella Alsan and Marianne Wanamaker found that disclosure of the study was followed by reduced medical contact among older Black men and worse health outcomes, a real and lasting cost (Alsan and Wanamaker 2018).
But Vanessa Northington Gamble and Susan Reverby press a sharp objection to the standard account. Black medical distrust did not begin with Tuskegee and is not exhausted by it; it tracks a long history and, more importantly, present mistreatment — documented disparities in pain treatment and outcomes, structural barriers in care now (Gamble 1997). To explain today’s distrust by pointing only to a study that ended in 1972 can become a way of locating the problem safely in the past, and of casting Black patients as irrationally haunted rather than accurately observant. (Reverby also notes a precise irony: the Tuskegee men were not distrustful refusers — they trusted the government doctors, which is exactly how they were used.) The COVID-19 vaccination gap became the live test of this dispute: was the right response to recite Tuskegee and reassure, or to address the present-day inequities that make wariness a reasonable inference?
What actually moved the gap is instructive. Early 2021 messaging that leaned on history-and-reassurance — that was then, the rules are different now, the vaccine is safe — tended to land poorly, partly because it answered a question many Black patients were not asking. A review of equity strategies found that the interventions that raised uptake were structural and relational rather than rhetorical: vaccine sites placed inside trusted Black churches and barbershops, and outreach delivered by community health workers, clergy, and Black clinicians rather than by the institutions themselves (Dada et al. 2022). The recurring mechanism is concordance — patients are more likely to accept advice, including vaccination, from clinicians who share their background or have earned standing in their community. A randomized field experiment in Oakland found that Black men assigned to a Black physician agreed to substantially more preventive services, including invasive screening, than those assigned to a non-Black physician — a gap that no recitation of history opens or closes (Alsan et al. 2019). The lesson tracks Reverby’s point precisely: the operative variable was not how vividly the past was explained but how much present trustworthiness the patient could actually observe in the room.
That gives a clinician something to do rather than something to recite. It does not, however, settle the argument — it arguably concedes the critique’s premise (trust follows present conditions, not historical correction) while leaving the standard view’s strongest reply intact: history is part of why those present conditions are read the way they are, and a patient who invokes Tuskegee is often naming a pattern, not a date — distrust as a response to everyday racism, not only to one archived study (Bajaj and Stanford 2021). The dispute is which description should drive the response.

The Distrust Dispute

If historical events like Tuskegee are the primary cause of medical distrust, then distrust should be solved by historical education and apology.
Medical distrust is primarily caused by ongoing, present-day systemic medical racism and disparities.
Therefore, medical distrust can only be solved by changing present-day medical practices, not just explaining the past.


Fifty years on, that is where the argument rests — not at a verdict, but at a question that a nurse, a researcher, and a public-health officer each have to answer the next time someone says, reasonably, that they are not sure they trust the people offering to help them.

title: The Tuskegee and Guatemala Syphilis Studies: Research Ethics, Whistleblowing, and Medical Distrust
text:
Discussion Questions

Reconstruct The Regulatory-Gap Argument and The Conscience Argument. They disagree about one premise — identify it, and say what Guatemala (eminent PI, NIH review, approving Surgeon General) contributes as evidence for one side.
Nurse Eunice Rivers was trusted by the men precisely because she was caring, constant, and one of their own — and that trust kept them in the study for decades. You are a nurse who has just realized the “treatment” you administer is a deception. What do you owe these particular patients that the institution does not, and what concretely do you do — knowing what the evidence says happens to nurses who report?
The Justice Reframing claims the deepest wrong was whom they chose, not the missing consent form. If a future study obtained perfect informed consent from poor, uninsured volunteers offered money they badly needed, would it have fixed Tuskegee’s wrong or only relocated it? Defend your answer using fair subject selection and Kipnis’s idea of vulnerability as pressure.
Apply The Institutional-Design Reply to a hospital you know. Name one concrete change (not an ethics hotline) that would make a dissenting nurse or resident less alone, and explain, using Milgram, why it would work.
A patient eligible for a new vaccine tells you she does not trust it, and mentions Tuskegee. Using The Distrust Dispute, give the strongest version of both responses — the reassure-and-educate response and the address-present-conditions response — and say which you would actually give her, and why.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Part A explored the biological development of the fetus alongside major candidate criteria for establishing moral status in clinical ethics.
We examined foundational moral frameworks including Judith Jarvis Thomson’s violinist analogy, Don Marquis’s future-like-ours (FLO) account, and Rosalind Hursthouse’s virtue ethics framework.
Part B transitions from pure moral theory to investigate the concrete legal, historical, and global policies that govern reproduction.
Our primary inquiry centers on how governments regulate individual reproduction, and where these state interests clash with individual bodily autonomy.





By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Tracing the legal trajectory of reproduction from ancient exposure practices and English Common Law quickening standards to the U.S. post-Dobbs landscape.
Analyze the foundational legal concepts of Common Law, precedent, and stare decisis as they apply to healthcare.
Compare coercive and non-coercive demographic state policies (China’s One-Child Policy vs. Romania’s Decree 770 and Singapore’s incentive package).
Apply the four principles (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to post-Dobbs clinical exceptions and moral distress.












Who owns the cradle?
Imagine a state facing severe demographic collapse that passes a law making pregnancy and childbirth a mandatory public service for all compatible citizens.

If a country is facing severe population collapse, does the government have a right to encourage or push citizens to have more children, or is family size a purely private choice?
Where is the boundary between a helpful public health incentive (like child care funding) and an unfair level of state control over reproduction?
How would you weigh a state’s concern for its future survival against an individual’s right to control their own body?





Use this thought experiment to set the theme: reproductive rights are not just about personal moral choices, but about the historical boundary lines between individual liberty and state authority.










Note



Three starting points are crucial for our analysis today:

State reproductive interests are historically fluid and have shifted between demanding more births or fewer births based on economic utility.
The law functions as an ethical instrument that institutionalizes specific balances of autonomy, paternalism, and justice.
Vague statutory codes often fracture when they collide with the rapid, continuous clinical realities of emergency medicine.

Note

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.1 Exposure & Infanticide in Antiquity



2.1.1 The Historical Reality

In ancient Roman and Greek societies, the abandonment or exposure of unwanted newborn infants was widely tolerated and legally practiced.
This practice served as the primary mechanism for family planning, population control, and managing congenital anomalies.






2.1.2 The Patria Potestas Model

Under Roman law, the male head of the household held absolute legal authority over all family members, which included the power of life and death.
A newborn did not possess inherent legal status at birth; protection was granted only when the father formally accepted the child into the household.





Point out that in antiquity, the boundary of legal and moral protection did not sit at fertilization, neural activity, or birth. It sat at paternal acceptance.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.2 The Shift to Moral Status

The rise of Judeo-Christian ethics in the late Roman Empire led to the gradual outlawing of infant exposure and infanticide.
These moral shifts pushed the threshold of legal protection backward from paternal acceptance to the moment of birth.
The newborn was recognized for the first time as an independent legal subject owed protection against the household’s immediate utility.
This historical transition established the precedent that the state holds a fundamental duty to protect vulnerable nascent life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.3 English Common Law & “Quickening”

Under English Common Law, abortion was not treated as a crime or misdemeanor prior to the biological boundary of quickening (Mohr 1978).
Quickening is defined as the moment when a pregnant person first feels the fetus move in the womb, typically occurring between 16 and 20 weeks.
Before quickening occurred, there was no objective evidence of independent life under common law understanding, meaning early termination was legally neutral.
Once quickening was documented, performing an abortion was treated as a misdemeanor rather than a capital offense.


Emphasize that the quickening standard represents a subjective, patient-centered boundary of legal status rather than an abstract biological marker.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.4 Early American Law & Advertised Midwifery

Early American jurisdictions inherited and fully adopted the English Common Law quickening standard.
Prior to the mid-19th century, early-term abortifacient medications and services were openly advertised and sold in U.S. newspapers.
Reproductive healthcare was largely managed within the home by patients, midwives, and herbalists without formal state interference.
Before 1840, no state in the union had enacted statutory bans on early-term abortion.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
2.5 The 19th-Century Professionalization Shift

The newly formed American Medical Association (AMA) launched a aggressive national campaign in 1847 to criminalize abortion at all stages (Mohr 1978).
Elite physicians utilized this campaign to establish monopoly control over medical practice, successfully squeezing out midwives and homeopaths.
Physicians argued that biological life was continuous from fertilization, making the common law quickening threshold scientifically arbitrary.
By 1880, every U.S. state had enacted statutes criminalizing abortion at all stages, shifting reproductive control from the home to state-licensed doctors.


Detail the dual motives of the AMA campaign: genuine scientific conviction about embryonic continuity alongside professional self-preservation and market control.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 2.6 History Debrief: Lessons from the Past
text:
2.6 History Debrief: Lessons from the Past

How does history complicate modern debates?

Knowing that early-stage abortion was widely accepted in early American history, does this change how we should view modern laws, or should we focus strictly on our current moral values?
How should we balance the AMA’s historical professional medical arguments against the traditional, patient-controlled care managed by midwives?
When a society decides to expand legal protection to new groups (like moving from historical infanticide to protecting newborns and fetuses), what factors usually drive that change?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.1 Statutes vs. Common Law



3.1.1 Statutory Law

Written laws are enacted directly by legislative bodies such as Congress or state legislatures.
These codes are precise, prospective, and codify specific rules, prohibitions, and penalties.
An example is a state legislature passing a statutory code banning abortion after six weeks.






3.1.2 Common Law

Case law is developed dynamically by judges through written decisions of courts.
Rulings are iterative, retrospective, and based on the historical accumulation of decisions.
An example is the court-developed duty-to-warn standard in modern psychiatry (Tarasoff).





Intro students often confuse these two. Emphasize that the U.S. healthcare system sits at the intersection of both statutory codes and judicial common law rulings.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.2 Precedent Defined

Legal Precedent
A precedent is a principle or rule established in a previous legal case that is either binding on or persuasive for a court when deciding subsequent cases with similar facts.


Lower courts must follow the binding precedents set by higher courts within their specific jurisdiction.
Higher courts generally follow their own prior precedents to maintain horizontal consistency and legal integrity.
Precedents allow individuals, hospitals, and clinicians to predict the legal consequences of their professional clinical actions.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.3 The Principle of Stare Decisis

The legal principle of stare decisis translates literally to “stand by things decided.”
Under this rule, courts must adhere to prior precedents and not lightly overrule established rulings.
This conservative standard prevents the law from changing rapidly with every political shift or judicial appointment.
Adhering to prior rulings ensures that citizens can rely on the law as a stable and predictable framework over decades.


Explain that stare decisis is not an absolute rule, but a powerful judicial presumption in favor of consistency over individual correction.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 3.4 The Moral Weight of Reliance Interests
text:
3.4 The Moral Weight of Reliance Interests

Citizens develop legitimate expectations and structure their lives, educations, and careers relying on established precedents.
Over decades, individuals made long-term decisions about families and careers relying on the availability of reproductive autonomy.
Rulings that overrule long-standing precedents directly disrupt these reliance interests, particularly impacting women’s participation in public life.
To overrule a precedent, a court must justify why correcting a perceived error is worth disrupting massive reliance interests.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.5 Overturning Precedent: The High Bar

High courts can and do overturn prior precedents if a ruling is judged fundamentally unworkable or unjust.
The court examines whether the precedent is clear enough for lower courts to apply consistently, or if it generates endless confusion.
Judges consider whether medical science, technology, or societal understanding have evolved to make the old rule obsolete.
A famous historical example is the Supreme Court overturning the segregationist precedent of Plessy v. Ferguson in Brown v. Board of Education.


Use Brown v. Board to show that overturning precedent is sometimes morally mandatory, but requires meeting a heavy burden of justification.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 3.6 Precedent in Action: Casey’s Defense of Roe
text:
3.6 Precedent in Action: Casey’s Defense of Roe

In Planned Parenthood v. Casey, a newly appointed conservative-leaning court was widely expected to overrule Roe v. Wade (US Supreme Court 1992).
Justices O’Connor, Kennedy, and Souter refused to overrule Roe, basing their entire decision on stare decisis and reliance interests.
The court ruled that overruling under pressure would subvert the judiciary’s legitimacy and disrupt societal reliance on reproductive liberty.
The compromise preserved the essential holding of Roe (pre-viability access) while permitting more pre-viability regulations.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.7 Precedent Overturned: Dobbs’ Reasoning

In Dobbs v. Jackson, the Supreme Court overruled both Roe and Casey entirely, ending fifty years of federal constitutional protection (US Supreme Court 2022).
Justice Alito argued that Roe was egregiously wrong from the day it was decided, violating originalist constitutional limits.
The majority claimed that Roe’s trimester framework was unworkable and had failed to produce a stable societal settlement.
Alito argued that because Roe withdrew protection from what many citizens consider a human person, reliance interests could not protect a wrongful precedent.


Highlight Alito’s point: stare decisis cannot be used to protect a precedent that the current court judges to involve the unjust termination of potential human life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
3.8 Legal Literacy Review

Predictability vs. Correction

In your own life, how important is it that rules remain stable and predictable over time? Why might a court choose to stand by an old decision even if it thinks the original reasoning was flawed?
How do people rely on long-standing legal rights when making major plans like choosing a career, getting married, or deciding when to start a family?
If key laws can be reversed whenever new judges are appointed, does that make the court look like a neutral legal referee or a political body? How can a legal system protect its credibility?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.1 Three Visions of Liberty

Roe v. Wade (1973) defined liberty as privacy and autonomy under the Due Process Clause, protected by a biological trimester grid (US Supreme Court 1973).
Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992) defined liberty as relational reliance and self-definition, protected by the “undue burden” standard (US Supreme Court 1992).
Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) defined liberty as strictly bounded by history and democratic federalism, returning reproductive control to local state legislatures (US Supreme Court 2022).


Use this slide to group these three cases conceptually. Frame them as three completely distinct philosophical definitions of what “liberty” means under the constitution.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.2 Model 1: The Trimester / Privacy Model (Roe)



4.2.1 The Core Holding

Privacy Standard: The 14th Amendment’s liberty guarantee includes a fundamental right to privacy that covers reproductive decisions (US Supreme Court 1973).
Strict Scrutiny: Any state law restricting this right must meet the highest bar of constitutional review.






4.2.2 The Trimester Grid

Trimester 1: Autonomy dominates. The state cannot restrict abortion; decisions rest solely with patient and doctor.
Trimester 2: State interest in patient health grows. Regulations allowed only to protect maternal health.
Trimester 3 (Viability): State interest in potential life becomes compelling. Banned entirely except to save maternal life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: 4.3 Model 2: The Viability / Undue Burden Model (Casey)
text:
4.3 Model 2: The Viability / Undue Burden Model (Casey)

Casey abandoned the trimester grid, establishing viability as the single moral and legal threshold (US Supreme Court 1992).
Before viability, the state may regulate abortion to protect potential life, provided the regulation does not create an undue burden.
An undue burden is defined as a regulation that has the purpose or effect of placing a substantial obstacle in the path of a patient seeking an abortion.
After viability, the state’s interest is compelling, and it may ban abortion entirely, provided it has exceptions for maternal life and health.


Point out that Casey lowered the level of constitutional protection from “strict scrutiny” to “undue burden,” paving the way for targeted restrictions (TRAP laws).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.5 Model 3: The Democratic / History Model (Dobbs)

Dobbs declared that the U.S. Constitution does not confer a right to abortion, overruling Roe and Casey entirely (US Supreme Court 2022).
Justice Alito held that any constitutional right not explicitly named in the text must be “deeply rooted in this Nation’s history and tradition.”
Because 19th-century common law and state statutes criminalized abortion, Alito concluded it lacks any constitutional basis.
The authority to regulate, ban, or protect abortion is returned entirely to the elected representatives of each individual state.


Highlight that Dobbs changes the ethical forum: abortion is no longer a question of constitutional rights managed by federal courts, but a political question settled by state-by-state elections.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
4.6 Comparative Grid: U.S. Constitutional Arc









Legal Model / Case
Constitutional Basis
Core Legal Threshold
State Regulatory Power




Roe v. Wade (1973)
14th Amendment Privacy
Trimesters (1st, 2nd, 3rd)
Banned in Trimester 1; regulated in 2 for health; bans allowed in 3


Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992)
